elements.sent-2411-001: In this paper we derive formulae for the semiclassical tunneling in the presence of a constant magnetic field in $2$ dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-002: The `wells' in the problem are identical discs with Neumann boundary conditions, so we study the magnetic Neumann Laplacian in the complement of a set of discs.
elements.sent-2411-003: We provide a reduction method to an interaction matrix, which works for a general configuration of obstacles.
elements.sent-2411-004: When there are two discs, we deduce an asymptotic formula for the spectral gap.
elements.sent-2411-005: When the discs are placed along a regular lattice, we derive an effective operator which gives rise to the famous Harper's equation.
elements.sent-2411-006: Main challenges in this problem compared to recent results on magnetic tunneling are the fact that one-well ground states have non-trivial angular momentum which depends on the semiclassical parameter, and the existence of eigenvalue crossings.
elements.sent-2411-007: This article is devoted to the spectral analysis of the magnetic Laplacian \begin{equation}\Lh = \Big(-ih \partial_{x_1} + \frac{B x_2}{2} \Big)^2 + \Big(-ih \partial_{x_2} - \frac{B x_1}{2} \Big)^2,\end{equation} acting on domains of $\R^2$ of the form \begin{equation}\Omega_V = \R^2 \setminus \Big( \bigcup_{\alpha \in V} D(\alpha,R) \Big),\end{equation} where $R>0$ is a fixed radius, and $V$ is a discrete set of points.
elements.sent-2411-008: We assume that the obstacles $D(\alpha,R)$ are disjoint, and we denote by $\Lh^V$ the magnetic Neumann realization of $\Lh$ on $\Omega_V$.
elements.sent-2411-009: When the magnetic field is very large -- which is equivalent to the semiclassical limit $h \to 0^+$ -- we know that there are bound states concentrating along the boundaries of the obstacles, which decay exponentially fast in the distance to the obstacles.

elements.section-2411-006: tunneling for a lattice of obstacles
elements.sent-2411-371: In this section be calculate the tunneling in the case of infinitely many disjoint obstacles.
elements.sent-2411-372: We focus here on the special case when the obstacles are distributed along a lattice, that is $V= L \Z^2$.
elements.sent-2411-373: We use Theorem~\ref{thm.Gram , and make the specific choice $\varepsilon = 0.4$.}
elements.sent-2411-374: Indeed, we want $\varepsilon < \sqrt{2 -1$ to ensure that we do not capture the interaction between obstacles along diagonals.}
elements.sent-2411-375: For instance, the lattice points $(0,0)$ and $(L,L)$ have distance $L\sqrt{2 > L(1+ \varepsilon)$ to each other: their interaction is not included in the infinite matrix $\mathbf W$.}
elements.sent-2411-376: We also want $\varepsilon$ as large as possible in order to reduce the size of the error terms $\mathcal E_{\varepsilon,L}$.
elements.sent-2411-377: Note that we could also choose $\varepsilon$ larger and take into account the diagonal interactions, but this would make the interaction matrix $\mathbf W$ more complicated.
